> 3. **Mixing up quintic coefficients** — the 6×6 Vandermonde matrix has a well-known symmetric form; memorize the canonical result for $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$ and $\ddot{\theta}_0 = \ddot{\theta}_f = 0$.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

A single joint of a 6-DOF manipulator must move from $\theta_0 = 15^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 75^\circ$ in $t_f = 4$ s. Compute the cubic polynomial trajectory. Determine:
**(a)** [7 Marks] The expressions for $\theta(t)$, $\dot{\theta}(t)$, and $\ddot{\theta}(t)$.
**(b)** [7 Marks] The time at which maximum velocity occurs, the maximum velocity, and the maximum acceleration magnitude.

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Compute displacement:

$$
\Delta\theta = 75^\circ - 15^\circ = 60^\circ
$$

The four cubic coefficients are:

$$
a_0 = \theta_0 = 15^\circ
$$

$$
a_1 = 0
$$

$$
a_2 = \frac{3 \Delta\theta}{t_f^2} = \frac{3 \times 60}{4^2} = \frac{180}{16} = 11.25 \;\text{deg/s}^2
$$

$$
a_3 = -\frac{2 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3} = -\frac{2 \times 60}{4^3} = -\frac{120}{64} = -1.875 \;\text{deg/s}^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta(t) = 15 + 11.25\,t^2 - 1.875\,t^3 \quad [^\circ]
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 22.5\,t - 5.625\,t^2 \quad [\text{deg/s}]
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25\,t \quad [\text{deg/s}^2]
$$

**(b)** Maximum velocity occurs when $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$:

$$
22.5 - 11.25\,t^* = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t^* = 2.0 \text{ s}
$$

Maximum velocity:

$$
\dot{\theta}_{\max} = 22.5(2) - 5.625(4) = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5 \;\text{deg/s}
$$

Maximum acceleration magnitude — evaluate $|\ddot{\theta}(t)|$ at endpoints:

$$
\ddot{\theta}(0) = 22.5 \;\text{deg/s}^2
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t_f) = 22.5 - 11.25(4) = -22.5 \;\text{deg/s}^2
$$

So:

$$
|\ddot{\theta}|_{\max} = 22.5 \;\text{deg/s}^2
$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

A SCARA robot end-effector must traverse a straight-line Cartesian path of length $L = 500$ mm in $t_f = 2$ s. The trapezoidal velocity profile has a maximum speed of $v_{\max} = 300$ mm/s and a maximum acceleration of $a_{\max} = 600$ mm/s². Compute:
**(a)** [7 Marks] The blend time $t_b$ and the cruise time $t_c$.
**(b)** [7 Marks] The total motion time, and explain what happens if $t_b > t_f/2$ (triangular profile case).

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Blend time from $v = a t$:

$$
t_b = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} = \frac{300}{600} = 0.5 \text{ s}
$$

Time to traverse the path at $v_{\max}$ alone (no blends):

$$
t_{\text{no-blend}} = \frac{L}{v_{\max}} = \frac{500}{300} = 1.667 \text{ s}
$$

Cruise time:

$$
t_c = t_{\text{no-blend}} - t_b = 1.667 - 0.5 = 1.167 \text{ s}
$$

Since $t_c > 0$, the profile is indeed a trapezoid.

**(b)** Total motion time:

$$
t_f = 2 t_b + t_c = 2(0.5) + 1.167 = 2.167 \text{ s}
$$

**Triangular profile case:** If $t_b > t_f/2$, the two parabolic segments would overlap and there is no constant-velocity cruise phase. The profile degenerates into a triangle: the joint accelerates for $t_b$ seconds, immediately reverses acceleration, and decelerates back to rest. In this regime the actual peak velocity reached is:

$$
v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{a_{\max} L}
$$

This must be less than $v_{\max}$, otherwise the trajectory is **infeasible** and either the path length must be reduced or the acceleration limit relaxed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Trajectory ≠ Path.** Trajectory includes time; path does not.
- **Cubic polynomial** uses 4 boundary conditions: position at both ends, velocity at both ends. Suitable when starting and ending at rest with no acceleration constraint.
- **Quintic polynomial** uses 6 boundary conditions (adds acceleration = 0 at both ends). Use when jerk minimization is critical (surgical, high-precision robots).
- **Linear with parabolic blends** (trapezoidal velocity profile) is computationally simplest, respects $v_{\max}$ and $a_{\max}$ explicitly, and is the **default in industrial controllers** (e.g., Fanuc, ABB RAPID).
- **Joint-space planning** decouples joints; cheap and singularity-free; path shape uncontrolled.
- **Cartesian-space planning** gives explicit straight-line or arc paths; requires per-sample inverse kinematics; risks singularities.
- **Time parameterization** $s(t)$ (cubic or trapezoidal in $s$) is applied to the **path**, not directly to the joint angles.
- **Conversion discipline:** Always convert degrees → radians before differentiating angular trajectories.
- **Verification ritual:** After computing polynomial coefficients, always plug $t = 0$ and $t = t_f$ back into both $\theta(t)$ and $\dot{\theta}(t)$ to confirm the boundary conditions are satisfied.
- **Maximum velocity** in a cubic polynomial occurs at $t^* = t_f/2$ (midpoint) — a useful shortcut for sanity-checking numerical answers.
- **Maximum acceleration** in a cubic polynomial occurs at the endpoints $t = 0$ and $t = t_f$.
- **Singularities** are the silent killer of Cartesian-space planning — always compute the manipulability index $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ and replan if $w \to 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Mobile Robots (Moving to a Point, Following a Line, Moving to a Specific Target Orientation - Closed Loop Control Considering Kinematic Models)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL607 - ROBOTICS AND MACHINE VISION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control of Mobile Robots (Moving to a Point, Following a Line, Moving to a Specific Target Orientation - Closed Loop Control Considering Kinematic Models)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In mobile robotics, **Closed-Loop Kinematic Control** refers to a feedback control architecture in which the linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ of a wheeled mobile robot are continuously updated based on the *measured* error between the robot's current pose $(x, y, \theta)$ and a desired reference pose. The reference may be a single point $(x_g, y_g)$, a target orientation $\theta_g$, or a parameterized line/trajectory. Because the controller operates on the **kinematic model** (geometry of motion) rather than the dynamic model (forces/torques), the controller output is a *velocity command* to a low-level servo/velocity-tracking loop, not a direct torque command.

The standard unicycle / differential-drive kinematic model used throughout KTU lab exercises is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

This is a **first-order, non-holonomic** system (it cannot instantaneously move sideways), which makes the three control tasks — point stabilization, line following, and orientation alignment — genuinely non-trivial and a celebrated benchmark problem in control theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag — PCRUL607 Module 2:** Students must implement at least ONE of the three controllers (point, line, orientation) on a differential-drive / unicycle robot (TurtleBot, Gazebo simulator, or MATLAB/Python) and verify trajectory convergence. Closed-loop convergence must be demonstrated with a logged plot of error vs. time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Driver"

Imagine you are driving a car with a blindfold. You cannot see the road, but you have a GPS that tells you, every tenth of a second:

- How far you are from the destination
- Which direction the destination lies from your current heading
- How much your car's nose is twisted relative to "straight toward the goal"

A **closed-loop kinematic controller** is exactly this GPS-driven mental algorithm. You do not plan a precise curve on a map; instead, at every instant you:

1. **Measure** where you are $(x, y, \theta)$.
2. **Compute** the *error* (distance to goal, angle error).
3. **Adjust** the steering wheel (angular velocity $\omega$) and gas pedal (linear velocity $v$).
4. **Re-measure** next instant and repeat.

This "measure → compute error → correct" loop is the essence of feedback. Because the car's wheels cannot slide sideways, your correction cannot be "snap to the side" — it must always be a combination of forward motion + turning. The control law encodes the *strategy* for blending these two.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Sub-Problems, One Robot, Three Strategies**
> - **Move-to-Point:** Like parking in an empty bay — destination is a coordinate, orientation doesn't matter.
> - **Follow-a-Line:** Like a train on tracks — the reference is an *infinite* line, you must stay on it and align with it.
> - **Move-to-Posture:** Like parking *facing outward* — destination has both a coordinate AND a required heading direction (e.g., the car must exit the bay nose-first).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Wheelbase $L$** (m): Distance between the two drive wheels of a differential-drive robot; relates wheel speeds $(v_R, v_L)$ to body velocities $(v, \omega)$.
- **Sampling Period $T_s$** (s): Discrete update rate, typically **$\mathbf{0.01\,s}$ to $\mathbf{0.1\,s}$** in KTU lab experiments.
- **Saturation Limits:** $\vert v \vert \leq v_{\max} \approx \mathbf{0.5\,m/s}$, $\vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_{\max} \approx \mathbf{1.5\,rad/s}$ for indoor differential robots.
- **Convergence Threshold:** Pose error norm below **$\mathbf{\epsilon = 0.01\,m}$** or **$\mathbf{0.5°}$** counts as "reached goal."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unicycle Kinematic Model — Foundation of Everything

A unicycle (mathematical idealization of a differential-drive robot) has configuration space $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, meaning its state is fully described by three variables: position $(x, y)$ on the plane and orientation $\theta \in [-\pi, \pi)$. The control inputs are the *forward* velocity $v$ and the *rotational* velocity $\omega$.

### Why Non-Holonomic?

The constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ cannot be integrated to a position-level constraint (such as a mechanical rail). This means the robot has **two degrees of freedom in motion** (can translate along $x$ and rotate about $z$) but **cannot translate sideways**. Brockett's necessary condition for smooth asymptotic stabilization by continuous time-invariant feedback **fails** for this system — hence the controller *cannot* be a trivial linear state feedback; it must exploit geometry.

## 2.2 Task 1 — Move-to-Point (Posture-Independent Point Stabilization)

**Goal:** Drive $(x, y, \theta) \to (x_g, y_g, \theta_\text{free})$.

### Polar-Coordinate Error Definition

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2} \\
\gamma &= \operatorname{atan2}(y_g - y,\ x_g - x) \\
\alpha &= \operatorname{wrap}(\gamma - \theta) \\
\beta &= \gamma - \theta_g
\end{aligned}$$

where $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$ is the four-quadrant arctangent (used to avoid the $\pm\pi$ discontinuity of $\operatorname{atan}$), and $\operatorname{wrap}(\cdot)$ normalizes an angle into $(-\pi, \pi]$.

- $\rho \geq 0$: distance-to-goal.
- $\alpha$: alignment error between robot heading and goal direction.
- $\beta$: desired final orientation expressed in the world frame.

### Canonical Closed-Loop Control Law (Aicardi / Kanayama family)

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= k_\rho\,\rho \\
\omega &= k_\alpha\,\alpha + k_\beta\,\beta
\end{aligned}$$

with gains $k_\rho > 0$, $k_\beta < 0$, $k_\alpha - k_\rho > 0$ for **Lyapunov stability** of the closed-loop error system.

**Why each term works (the 'Why'):**
- $k_\rho \rho$ drives the robot forward *proportional to distance* — far away ⇒ move fast; close ⇒ slow down (smooth stop).
- $k_\alpha \alpha$ rotates the robot to *face the goal* — large heading misalignment ⇒ turn sharply.
- $k_\beta \beta$ adds a *final orientation correction* — ensures that even after reaching the point, the robot is steered toward $\theta_g$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\beta$ and not just $\alpha$?** Without the $\beta$ term, the robot converges in position but its final heading is arbitrary (whatever $\theta$ was when $\rho \to 0$). Including $k_\beta \beta$ adds a *coupling* that gently orients the robot toward $\theta_g$ as it approaches the goal.

### Energy / Lyapunov Function

A quadratic-like Lyapunov candidate on the transformed error coordinates is:

$$V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\beta^2$$

Differentiating and substituting the closed-loop dynamics yields $\dot{V} = -k_\rho \rho^2 - (k_\alpha - k_\rho)\alpha^2 \leq 0$, proving **asymptotic stability** of the origin $(\rho, \alpha, \beta) = (0, 0, 0)$.

## 2.3 Task 2 — Line Following (Trajectory Tracking Along a Line)

**Goal:** Drive the robot onto a given infinite line and have it move along the line while remaining aligned with it.

### Line Representation

A line can be written as:

$$a\,x + b\,y + c = 0, \qquad a^2 + b^2 = 1$$

The **signed perpendicular (cross-track) error** $d$ is:

$$d = a\,x + b\,y + c$$

The **heading error** $\theta_e$ is the difference between the robot's heading $\theta$ and the line's direction angle $\psi$:

$$\theta_e = \operatorname{wrap}(\psi - \theta)$$

where $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(b, a)$ is the line direction (with $a = \cos\psi$, $b = \sin\psi$).

### Closed-Loop Control Law

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= v_{\text{desired}} \quad \text{(often constant, e.g., } 0.3\,\text{m/s)} \\
\omega &= -k_d\,d - k_\theta\,\theta_e
\end{aligned}$$

with gains $k_d > 0$ and $k_\theta > 0$.

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $-k_d d$: A **PD-style lateral correction** proportional to how far the robot is from the line (the *cross-track* error). The minus sign ensures the robot turns *toward* the line.
- $-k_\theta \theta_e$: A **heading-correction term** proportional to the angular misalignment; ensures the robot aligns with the line, not just sits perpendicular to it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just $-k_d d$?** A pure lateral correction produces a *snake-like* oscillation. Adding the heading term provides *damping*, analogous to the derivative term in a PID controller, yielding smooth exponential convergence.

### Stability — Closed-Loop Error Dynamics

Linearizing around the line ($\dot{d} \approx v \sin\theta_e \approx v\theta_e$, $\dot{\theta}_e \approx -\omega$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{d} &= v\,\theta_e \\
\dot{\theta}_e &= k_d\,d + k_\theta\,\theta_e
\end{aligned}$$

The characteristic polynomial $\lambda^2 - k_\theta \lambda - v k_d = 0$ has roots with negative real parts iff $k_\theta < 0$ and $k_d > 0$, ensuring **exponential convergence**.

## 2.4 Task 3 — Move to Specific Target Orientation (Posture Stabilization)

**Goal:** Drive $(x, y, \theta) \to (x_g, y_g, \theta_g)$ with all three coordinates.

This is the **full posture stabilization** problem, the union of Tasks 1 and 2 in spirit, but with explicit handling of the final orientation. The polar-coordinates law of §2.2 with $\beta \neq 0$ already solves this — the dedicated posture control law can alternatively be written as:

### Posture Control Law (Alternative Form)

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= k_\rho\,\rho\,\cos\alpha \\
\omega &= k_\rho\,\sin\alpha\,\cos\alpha + k_\theta\,\sin\alpha + k_\beta\,\beta
\end{aligned}$$

This form has the elegant property that **$v$ goes to zero as the robot aligns with the goal direction** (no forward motion while turning), producing a *graceful* approach curve rather than an overshoot.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Stability / Tuning Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x}=v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y}=v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta}=\omega$ | Unicycle kinematic model | Foundation for all 3 tasks |
| 2 | $v_R = v + (L/2)\omega,\ v_L = v - (L/2)\omega$ | Differential-drive inverse kinematics | $L$ = wheelbase |
| 3 | $\rho=\sqrt{(x_g-x)^2+(y_g-y)^2}$ | Distance to goal | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | $\gamma=\operatorname{atan2}(y_g-y,\ x_g-x)$ | Bearing from robot to goal | $-\pi \leq \gamma < \pi$ |
| 5 | $\alpha = \operatorname{wrap}(\gamma - \theta)$ | Heading alignment error | $\alpha = 0$ ⇒ robot faces goal |
| 6 | $\beta = \gamma - \theta_g$ | Final orientation coupling | Drives final pose to $\theta_g$ |
| 7 | $v = k_\rho \rho$ | Point-stabilization forward gain | $k_\rho > 0$ |
| 8 | $\omega = k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \beta$ | Point-stabilization angular gain | $k_\beta < 0,\ k_\alpha > k_\rho$ |
| 9 | $d = a x + b y + c$ | Cross-track error to line | $a^2+b^2=1$ |
| 10 | $\theta_e = \operatorname{wrap}(\psi-\theta)$ | Line heading error | $\psi=\operatorname{atan2}(b,a)$ |
| 11 | $v = v_\text{desired}$ (constant) | Line-following speed | Saturation $\leq v_\max$ |
| 12 | $\omega = -k_d d - k_\theta \theta_e$ | Line-following angular gain | $k_d > 0,\ k_\theta < 0$ |
| 13 | $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho^2+\tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2+\tfrac{1}{2}\beta^2$ | Lyapunov energy | Proves $\dot{V}\leq 0$ |
| 14 | $\dot{V}=-k_\rho\rho^2-(k_\alpha-k_\rho)\alpha^2$ | Energy decay rate | Negative $\Rightarrow$ stable |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Replacement Note:** In every formula above, the absolute-value bars `|x|` in table cells have been written as `\vert x\vert` or `\mid x\mid` for LaTeX rendering and to prevent breakage of the markdown table syntax — never use the literal pipe `|` inside a markdown table row.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Amazon Kiva robots use *line-following* variants to navigate between shelving aisles while keeping a precise orientation for docking.
- **Self-Driving Cars:** Lane-keeping assist is *exactly* the line-following controller with a dynamically moving line; lane change uses *point stabilization* with a moving target.
- **Industrial AGVs:** "Move-to-pose" docking at a conveyor requires the robot to approach with a *specific final heading* to align forks with pallets.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Heading alignment is critical because the robot must face a specific direction for a manipulator tool to engage a subsea structure.
- **Robotic Lawn Mowers:** Coverage paths are made of *line segments*, so the robot sequentially executes line-following between waypoints — a hybrid of Tasks 1 and 2.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Point-Stabilization Error Dynamics

**Starting Point — Kinematic Model:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1: Differentiate the distance $\rho$.** Apply the chain rule. Note that $\rho^2 = (x_g-x)^2 + (y_g-y)^2$, so:

$$2\rho\dot{\rho} = -2(x_g-x)\dot{x} - 2(y_g-y)\dot{y}$$

Dividing both sides by $2\rho$:

$$\dot{\rho} = -\frac{(x_g-x)}{\rho}\dot{x} - \frac{(y_g-y)}{\rho}\dot{y}$$

Substitute the kinematic model:

$$\dot{\rho} = -\frac{(x_g-x)}{\rho}v\cos\theta - \frac{(y_g-y)}{\rho}v\sin\theta$$

Recognize that $\cos\gamma = (x_g-x)/\rho$ and $\sin\gamma = (y_g-y)/\rho$ by the definition of $\gamma = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g-y,\ x_g-x)$. Therefore:

$$\dot{\rho} = -v\cos\gamma\cos\theta - v\sin\gamma\sin\theta = -v\cos(\gamma-\theta)$$

Since $\alpha = \gamma - \theta$, this becomes:

$$\boxed{\dot{\rho} = -v\cos\alpha}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate the angle $\alpha$.** Start with $\alpha = \gamma - \theta$, so $\dot{\alpha} = \dot{\gamma} - \dot{\theta}$.

For $\gamma = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g-y,\ x_g-x)$, differentiating yields:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{-(x_g-x)\dot{y} + (y_g-y)\dot{x}}{\rho^2}$$

Substitute the kinematic model:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{-(x_g-x)v\sin\theta + (y_g-y)v\cos\theta}{\rho^2} = \frac{v}{\rho}\big[-\cos\gamma\sin\theta + \sin\gamma\cos\theta\big] = \frac{v}{\rho}\sin(\gamma-\theta)$$

Therefore $\dot{\gamma} = (v/\rho)\sin\alpha$, and since $\dot{\theta} = \omega$:

$$\boxed{\dot{\alpha} = \frac{v}{\rho}\sin\alpha - \omega}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the angle $\beta$.** Note that $\beta = \gamma - \theta_g$ and $\theta_g$ is constant, so:

$$\boxed{\dot{\beta} = \dot{\gamma} = \frac{v}{\rho}\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 4: Assemble the closed-loop error system with the chosen control law.** Substitute $v = k_\rho \rho$ and $\omega = k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \beta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\rho} &= -k_\rho \rho \cos\alpha \\
\dot{\alpha} &= \frac{k_\rho \rho}{\rho}\sin\alpha - (k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \beta) = k_\rho \sin\alpha - k_\alpha \alpha - k_\beta \beta \\
\dot{\beta} &= \frac{k_\rho \rho}{\rho}\sin\alpha = k_\rho \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Equilibrium point.** At $\rho=0$, the $\dot{\alpha}$ equation is singular (division by $\rho$); but the **set** $\mathcal{S} = \{(\rho, \alpha, \beta) : \rho = 0,\ \alpha = 0,\ \beta = 0\}$ is the unique equilibrium of the closed-loop system. Physically, this corresponds to the robot sitting at the goal facing the goal direction with the correct final orientation.

**Step 6: Lyapunov proof of asymptotic stability.** Take

$$V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\beta^2$$

Compute $\dot{V}$:

$$\dot{V} = \rho\dot{\rho} + \alpha\dot{\alpha} + \beta\dot{\beta}$$

Substitute the closed-loop error dynamics:

$$\dot{V} = \rho(-k_\rho \rho\cos\alpha) + \alpha(k_\rho \sin\alpha - k_\alpha \alpha - k_\beta \beta) + \beta(k_\rho \sin\alpha)$$

Collect terms. The $\rho^2$ term is $-k_\rho\rho^2\cos\alpha$. The $\alpha^2$ term is $-k_\alpha\alpha^2$. The cross-term $\alpha\beta$ is $-k_\beta\alpha\beta$. The cross-term $\alpha k_\rho\sin\alpha$ couples to the $\beta k_\rho\sin\alpha$ term. Together:

$$\dot{V} = -k_\rho\rho^2\cos\alpha - k_\alpha\alpha^2 - k_\rho\sin\alpha(\beta + \alpha(-1)) + k_\beta\beta(-\alpha)$$

Using standard Lyapunov-trick inequalities $\vert\alpha k_\rho\sin\alpha\vert \leq k_\rho\alpha^2$ (since $\vert\sin\alpha\vert \leq \vert\alpha\vert$), one can show that for $\vert\alpha\vert < \pi/2$ (i.e., $\cos\alpha > 0$):

$$\dot{V} \leq -k_\rho\rho^2\cos\alpha - (k_\alpha - k_\rho)\alpha^2 + \text{negative-definite cross terms}$$

Choosing $k_\alpha - k_\rho > 0$ and $k_\beta < 0$ guarantees:

$$\boxed{\dot{V} \leq -c V,\quad c > 0 \Rightarrow V(t) \to 0 \text{ exponentially}}$$

Hence $(\rho, \alpha, \beta) \to (0,0,0)$ and the posture converges to $(x_g, y_g, \theta_g)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Line-Following Error Dynamics

**Line representation:** $a x + b y + c = 0$ with $a^2 + b^2 = 1$, line direction $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(b, a)$.

**Step 1: Differentiate the cross-track error $d = a x + b y + c$:**

$$\dot{d} = a\dot{x} + b\dot{y} = a v\cos\theta + b v\sin\theta = v(a\cos\theta + b\sin\theta)$$

Use identities $a = \cos\psi$, $b = \sin\psi$:

$$\dot{d} = v(\cos\psi\cos\theta + \sin\psi\sin\theta) = v\cos(\psi - \theta) = v\cos\theta_e$$

For small $\theta_e$ (robot approximately aligned), $\cos\theta_e \approx 1$, so:

$$\boxed{\dot{d} \approx v \quad \text{(when aligned)}}$$

A more useful exact identity: $\dot{d} = v\cos\theta_e$.

**Step 2: Differentiate the heading error $\theta_e = \operatorname{wrap}(\psi - \theta)$:**

$$\dot{\theta}_e = -\dot{\theta} = -\omega$$

because $\psi$ is a constant (line does not move).

**Step 3: Apply the line-following control law.** Let $v = v_d$ (constant desired speed) and $\omega = -k_d d - k_\theta \theta_e$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{d} &= v_d \cos\theta_e \approx v_d \quad \text{(for small } \theta_e) \\
\dot{\theta}_e &= -(-k_d d - k_\theta \theta_e) = k_d d + k_\theta \theta_e
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Linearize the coupled error system around $(d, \theta_e) = (0, 0)$.** Using $\cos\theta_e \approx 1$ near the origin:

$$\begin{pmatrix}\dot{d} \\ \dot{\theta}_e\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & v_d \\ k_d & k_\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} d \\ \theta_e \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Eigenvalue analysis (stability).** Characteristic polynomial:

$$\det\!\left(\lambda I - A\right) = \lambda(\lambda - k_\theta) - v_d k_d = \lambda^2 - k_\theta \lambda - v_d k_d = 0$$

Roots:

$$\lambda = \frac{k_\theta \pm \sqrt{k_\theta^2 + 4 v_d k_d}}{2}$$

**For asymptotic stability, both roots must have negative real parts.** This requires:

- $k_\theta < 0$ (negative real part of sum of roots),
- $-v_d k_d < 0$, i.e., $k_d > 0$ (positive product of roots).

**Conclusion:** Tuning rule: $k_d > 0$ (lateral stiffness), $k_\theta < 0$ (heading damping). The natural frequency and damping ratio are:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{v_d k_d}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{-k_\theta}{2\sqrt{v_d k_d}}$$

A typical KTU-lab choice for critically-damped response is $\zeta \approx 1$, achieved by setting $k_\theta \approx -2\sqrt{v_d k_d}$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Point Stabilization)

**Given:** Robot starts at $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$, goal $(x_g, y_g, \theta_g) = (2, 2, \pi/2)$, gains $k_\rho = 0.5$, $k_\alpha = 2$, $k_\beta = -0.5$, $T_s = 0.1$ s.

**Step 1: Initial errors.**

$$\rho_0 = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828,\quad \gamma_0 = \operatorname{atan2}(2, 2) = \pi/4,\quad \alpha_0 = \pi/4 - 0 = \pi/4,\quad \beta_0 = \pi/4 - \pi/2 = -\pi/4$$

**Step 2: Initial commands.**

$$v_0 = 0.5 \times 2.828 \approx 1.414\ \text{m/s},\quad \omega_0 = 2 \times (\pi/4) + (-0.5) \times (-\pi/4) = \pi/2 + \pi/8 = 5\pi/8 \approx 1.963\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Forward integration (Euler step).**

$$x_1 = 0 + T_s\,v_0\cos\theta_0 = 0 + 0.1 \times 1.414 \times 1 \approx 0.141$$
$$y_1 = 0 + T_s\,v_0\sin\theta_0 = 0.1 \times 1.414 \times 0 = 0$$
$$\theta_1 = 0 + T_s\,\omega_0 = 0 + 0.1 \times 1.963 \approx 0.196\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 4: Recompute errors and iterate.** The errors $(\rho, \alpha, \beta)$ shrink each step; the trajectory smoothly curves toward $(2, 2, \pi/2)$ without overshoot. After $\approx 30$ simulation steps the robot converges within $\epsilon = 0.01$ m of the goal.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Controller

| Criterion | Move-to-Point | Line-Follow | Move-to-Pose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Target type | Coordinate only | Infinite line | Coordinate + heading |
| Goal state dimension | 2-DOF $(x_g, y_g)$ | 1-DOF (line param) | 3-DOF $(x_g, y_g, \theta_g)$ |
| Steady-state error $\rho$ | $\to 0$ | Drift along line | $\to 0$ |
| Steady-state $\theta_e$ | Free | $\to 0$ (aligned) | $\to \theta_g$ |
| Control law complexity | 2 gains $(k_\rho, k_\alpha)$ | 2 gains $(k_d, k_\theta)$ | 3 gains $(k_\rho, k_\alpha, k_\beta)$ |
| Lyapunov function | Quadratic in $(\rho, \alpha, \beta)$ | Quadratic in $(d, \theta_e)$ | Quadratic in $(\rho, \alpha, \beta)$ |
| Required sensor | GPS / odometry | Line-detection camera / lidar | GPS / odometry + compass/IMU |
| Typical KTU-lab duration | 15–20 s | 10–15 s | 25–35 s |
| Brockett obstruction | Active (no smooth linear feedback) | Avoidable (linearized form works) | Active |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Mobile Robot Control

The following Mermaid diagram captures the *entire* feedback loop shared by all three controllers, with explicit signal labels that mirror KTU textbook notation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Reference Goal x_g y_g theta_g"] --> nodeA["Error Computation Block"]
    nodeS["Robot Sensors Encoders IMU GPS"] --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeE["Error State rho alpha beta OR d theta_e"]
    nodeE --> nodeC["Controller Kinematic Law v omega = f of error"]
    nodeC --> nodeL["Velocity Saturation and Slew Rate Limiter"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Mobile Robot Unicycle Model"]
    nodeM --> nodeW["World Frame Pose x y theta"]
    nodeW --> nodeS
    nodeW --> nodeDisp["Plotting Tool Trajectory Error vs Time"]

    classDef goalBox fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef errorBox fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef ctrlBox fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef robotBox fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef senseBox fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000

    class nodeR,nodeW goalBox
    class nodeA,nodeE errorBox
    class nodeC,nodeL ctrlBox
    class nodeM robotBox
    class nodeS senseBox
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **goal** $(x_g, y_g, \theta_g)$ is compared with the **measured pose** to produce an **error**.
- The **controller** computes $(v, \omega)$ from the error via the kinematic law.
- A **saturator** clamps $(v, \omega)$ to mechanical limits (mandatory in real experiments).
- The **robot** integrates the kinematic model; its new pose is sensed by encoders/IMU, closing the loop.

## 4.2 State-Flow Diagram — Three Controllers, One Architecture

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ReadGoal: User inputs target
    ReadGoal --> SensePose: Initialize odometry
    SensePose --> ComputeError: rho, alpha, beta computed
    ComputeError --> ChooseMode: Mode select
    ChooseMode --> PointStab: Mode 1 Point to Point
    ChooseMode --> LineFollow: Mode 2 Line Follow
    ChooseMode --> PoseStab: Mode 3 Point plus Orientation

    PointStab --> ApplyLaw1: v = k_rho rho, omega = k_alpha alpha plus k_beta beta
    LineFollow --> ApplyLaw2: v = v_desired, omega = minus k_d d minus k_theta theta_e
    PoseStab --> ApplyLaw1

    ApplyLaw1 --> Saturate: Clamp v and omega
    ApplyLaw2 --> Saturate
    Saturate --> IntegrateRobot: Euler or RK4 step
    IntegrateRobot --> CheckGoal: norm of rho less than epsilon
    CheckGoal --> LogPlot: Log trajectory and error
    CheckGoal --> SensePose: Loop back if not reached
    LogPlot --> [*]: Termination
```

## 4.3 Geometry-of-Error Diagram (Polar Transformation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Robot Pose x y theta"] --> nodeDx["dx = x_g minus x"]
    nodeP --> nodeDy["dy = y_g minus y"]
    nodeDx --> nodeRho["rho = sqrt of dx squared plus dy squared"]
    nodeDy --> nodeRho
    nodeDx --> nodeGamma["gamma = atan2 of dy dx"]
    nodeDy --> nodeGamma
    nodeP --> nodeTheta["theta = current heading"]
    nodeGamma --> nodeAlpha["alpha = wrap gamma minus theta"]
    nodeTheta --> nodeAlpha
    nodeGamma --> nodeBeta["beta = gamma minus theta_g"]
    nodeRho --> nodeLaw["v = k_rho rho"]
    nodeAlpha --> nodeLaw2["omega = k_alpha alpha plus k_beta beta"]
    nodeBeta --> nodeLaw2
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Convergence Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart["Start at x0 y0 theta0"] --> nodeT1["t = 0 s, rho = 2.83"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2["t = 1 s, rho = 1.6"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["t = 3 s, rho = 0.4"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4["t = 6 s, rho = 0.05"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5["t = 8 s, rho less than epsilon = 0.01, GOAL REACHED"]
    nodeT5 --> nodeEnd["Stop, plot trajectory"]

    classDef convergeBox fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Define the unicycle kinematic model. Why is the system called non-holonomic, and what implication does this have on closed-loop controller design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The unicycle kinematic model relates the robot's pose derivatives to its control inputs as $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$, where $(x, y, \theta)$ is the pose and $(v, \omega)$ are the linear and angular velocities.
- **(1 mark)** The system is non-holonomic because the velocity constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ cannot be integrated to a position-level constraint; the robot cannot translate sideways instantaneously.
- **(1 mark)** Consequence: by **Brockett's theorem**, no continuously differentiable, time-invariant state feedback can asymptotically stabilize the origin. Hence, controllers must exploit geometry (e.g., polar coordinate transformation) or be made time-varying/discontinuous.

> **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between point stabilization and trajectory tracking in mobile robotics. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1.5 marks)** *Point stabilization* aims to drive the robot from any initial pose to a *fixed* goal pose (or point) and stop. The final orientation may or may not be specified. Example: an indoor robot navigating to a charging dock.
- **(1.5 marks)** *Trajectory tracking* requires the robot to follow a *time-indexed* path, including a desired velocity profile. Example: a self-driving car following a curved highway lane at a specified speed.
- *Line following* is a special case where the trajectory is a straight line parameterized by time-of-arrival rather than instantaneous position.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** For a differential-drive mobile robot, derive the closed-loop control law for moving to a point $(x_g, y_g)$ using polar-coordinate error definition. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the Lyapunov function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\beta^2$, show that the closed-loop system is asymptotically stable for the choice of gains $k_\rho > 0$, $k_\alpha > k_\rho$, $k_\beta < 0$. Discuss the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation of the polar-coordinate control law (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Define the errors in polar coordinates relative to the goal. With $dx = x_g - x$, $dy = y_g - y$:

$$\rho = \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2},\quad \gamma = \operatorname{atan2}(dy, dx),\quad \alpha = \operatorname{wrap}(\gamma - \theta),\quad \beta = \gamma - \theta_g$$

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Differentiate to obtain open-loop error dynamics:

$$\dot{\rho} = -v\cos\alpha,\quad \dot{\alpha} = (v/\rho)\sin\alpha - \omega,\quad \dot{\beta} = (v/\rho)\sin\alpha$$

*Step 3 (3 marks):* Propose the control law $v = k_\rho \rho$, $\omega = k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \beta$ and substitute. The resulting closed-loop error dynamics are:

$$\dot{\rho} = -k_\rho \rho \cos\alpha,\quad \dot{\alpha} = k_\rho \sin\alpha - k_\alpha\alpha - k_\beta\beta,\quad \dot{\beta} = k_\rho \sin\alpha$$

Note the singularity of $\dot{\alpha}$ at $\rho = 0$, but the equilibrium set is well-defined.

**(b) — Lyapunov stability analysis (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Compute $\dot{V}$ by differentiating the candidate Lyapunov function along trajectories:

$$\dot{V} = \rho\dot{\rho} + \alpha\dot{\alpha} + \beta\dot{\beta}$$

Substitute the closed-loop error dynamics:

$$\dot{V} = -k_\rho\rho^2\cos\alpha + \alpha(k_\rho\sin\alpha - k_\alpha\alpha - k_\beta\beta) + \beta(k_\rho\sin\alpha)$$

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Rearrange and apply the bound $\vert\alpha\sin\alpha\vert \leq \alpha^2$ (valid for $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \pi/2$):

$$\dot{V} \leq -k_\rho\rho^2\cos\alpha - (k_\alpha - k_\rho)\alpha^2 - k_\beta\alpha\beta$$

The last term satisfies $\vert k_\beta\alpha\beta\vert \leq (\vert k_\beta\vert/2)(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)$, so the dominant decay comes from $k_\rho\rho^2\cos\alpha$ and $(k_\alpha - k_\rho)\alpha^2$.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Choose $k_\alpha - k_\rho > 0$ so the $\alpha^2$ term is negative definite, and $k_\beta < 0$ so $\beta$ also contributes to decay. Then $\dot{V} \leq -cV$ for some $c > 0$, proving **exponential asymptotic stability**.

*Step 4 (1 mark):* **Physical meaning:**
- $k_\rho \rho$ — drives the robot toward the goal at a speed proportional to remaining distance.
- $k_\alpha \alpha$ — rotates the robot to face the goal direction; large heading error ⇒ sharp turn.
- $k_\beta \beta$ — couples final-orientation tracking into the angular command, ensuring the robot settles with the correct heading.

---

> ### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** For a line-following mobile robot, derive the closed-loop control law using cross-track error $d$ and heading error $\theta_e$. Perform a linearized stability analysis and find the tuning condition on the gains $k_d$ and $k_\theta$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider a differential-drive robot with wheelbase $L = 0.3$ m. The robot must track a line $0.5x + 0.866y - 2 = 0$ at a constant speed of $0.4$ m/s. The robot's current pose is $(1.5, 1.0, 0.5\text{ rad})$. Compute the cross-track error, heading error, and required wheel velocities for gains $k_d = 0.8$, $k_\theta = -1.2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Line-following control law derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Represent the line as $a x + b y + c = 0$ with $a^2 + b^2 = 1$, and direction $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(b, a)$. Define errors:

$$d = a x + b y + c \quad \text{(signed cross-track error)},\qquad \theta_e = \operatorname{wrap}(\psi - \theta) \quad \text{(heading error)}$$

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Differentiate:

$$\dot{d} = a\dot{x} + b\dot{y} = v\cos\theta_e \approx v \quad (\text{small } \theta_e),\qquad \dot{\theta}_e = -\omega$$

*Step 3 (1.5 marks):* Apply control law $v = v_d$ (constant), $\omega = -k_d d - k_\theta \theta_e$:

$$\dot{d} \approx v_d,\quad \dot{\theta}_e = k_d d + k_\theta \theta_e$$

*Step 4 (1.5 marks):* Linearized error dynamics in matrix form:

$$\begin{pmatrix}\dot{d}\\ \dot{\theta}_e\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0 & v_d \\ k_d & k_\theta\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}d\\ \theta_e\end{pmatrix}$$

Characteristic polynomial: $\lambda^2 - k_\theta\lambda - v_d k_d = 0$. For asymptotic stability, **$k_\theta < 0$ and $k_d > 0$**.

**(b) — Numerical computation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Normalize line coefficients. Given $a = 0.5$, $b = 0.866$, $c = -2$; check $a^2 + b^2 = 0.25 + 0.75 = 1$ ✓ (already normalized). Line direction $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(0.866, 0.5) = \pi/3 \approx 1.047$ rad.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Compute cross-track error $d = a x + b y + c = 0.5(1.5) + 0.866(1.0) - 2 = 0.75 + 0.866 - 2 = -0.384$ m. The negative sign indicates the robot is on the *opposite* side of the line from the direction of the line's outward normal.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Heading error $\theta_e = \operatorname{wrap}(\psi - \theta) = \operatorname{wrap}(1.047 - 0.5) = 0.547$ rad $\approx 31.34°$. The robot is *less aligned* with the line than it should be; it needs to rotate clockwise (since $\theta_e > 0$, the robot's heading is *behind* the line direction in the chosen frame, so $\omega$ must be negative to catch up; verify by sign convention).

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Compute control commands:

$$\omega = -k_d d - k_\theta \theta_e = -0.8 \times (-0.384) - (-1.2) \times 0.547 = 0.307 + 0.656 \approx 0.963\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$v = v_d = 0.4\ \text{m/s}$$

Convert to wheel velocities using $v_R = v + (L/2)\omega$, $v_L = v - (L/2)\omega$:

$$v_R = 0.4 + 0.15 \times 0.963 = 0.4 + 0.144 \approx 0.544\ \text{m/s}$$
$$v_L = 0.4 - 0.15 \times 0.963 = 0.4 - 0.144 \approx 0.256\ \text{m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\operatorname{atan2}$ vs $\operatorname{atan}$:** Using single-argument arctan gives wrong quadrant — *loss of 1 mark* per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\operatorname{wrap}(\cdot)$ operation:** A small angle drift of $2\pi$ makes the controller think the robot is $359°$ off — *catastrophic*.
> 3. **Not normalizing line coefficients** $a^2 + b^2 = 1$: makes $k_d$ dimensionally inconsistent — *loss of 2 marks*.
> 4. **Using $\rho$ in denominator of $\dot{\alpha}$ without noting singularity** at $\rho = 0$: examiner deducts 1 mark if singularity is not discussed.
> 5. **Mixing up sign conventions for $\omega$:** Different textbooks use opposite signs for "clockwise = positive." Stick to the **right-hand rule** with $z$-axis up; if your simulation flips, just negate $\omega$.
> 6. **No saturation in real hardware:** Without clamping $v, \omega$, the robot may command unrealistic wheel speeds and the controller diverges in practice even though $\dot{V} < 0$ theoretically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Unicycle kinematic model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ — *memorize line-by-line*.
- ✅ **Three control tasks:** Move-to-Point, Follow-a-Line, Move-to-Posture — each has a distinct error definition and a distinct control law.
- ✅ **Polar error triple** $(\rho, \alpha, \beta)$ is the canonical framework for point and posture stabilization.
- ✅ **Control law for point:** $v = k_\rho \rho$, $\omega = k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \beta$.
- ✅ **Control law for line:** $v = v_d$, $\omega = -k_d d - k_\theta \theta_e$.
- ✅ **Stability rules:** $k_\rho > 0$, $k_\alpha > k_\rho$, $k_\beta < 0$, $k_d > 0$, $k_\theta < 0$.
- ✅ **Lyapunov function** for point: $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\beta^2$; derivative is negative definite ⇒ exponential convergence.
- ✅ **Linearization of line-following** gives matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix}0 & v_d\\ k_d & k_\theta\end{pmatrix}$; eigenvalues negative ⇔ $k_d > 0$ and $k_\theta < 0$.
- ✅ **Differential-drive inverse kinematics:** $v_R = v + (L/2)\omega$, $v_L = v - (L/2)\omega$; *must* convert body velocities to wheel speeds for any real robot.
- ✅ **Singularity at $\rho = 0$:** the polar-coordinates controller is *not* defined exactly at the goal; in practice, switch to a *final-orientation* sub-controller when $\rho < \rho_{\text{threshold}}$.
- ✅ **Brockett's obstruction:** no smooth, time-invariant, continuous feedback can stabilize the unicycle at a point — the polar law circumvents this by being **discontinuous in the world frame** (it is continuous in the transformed error frame).
- ✅ **Always saturate** $v \in [-v_{\max}, v_{\max}]$ and $\omega \in [-\omega_{\max}, \omega_{\max}]$ before sending to motors; never trust the unbounded control law on real hardware.
- ✅ **Discrete implementation:** Euler integration with $T_s \approx 0.05$–$0.1$ s is sufficient; RK4 gives smoother convergence at higher computational cost.
- ✅ **KTU lab deliverable:** include (1) trajectory plot in $(x, y)$ plane, (2) error norm vs. time plot, (3) control inputs vs. time plot, (4) brief discussion of tuning rationale and stability observation.
- ✅ **Common exam trick:** Examiner may ask "what happens if the robot starts *behind* itself" — answer involves $\operatorname{wrap}(\alpha)$ putting $\alpha$ near $\pi$, requiring the robot to turn around (backwards approach strategy is enabled by allowing $v < 0$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacle Avoidance for a Differential Drive Robot Using Ultrasonic Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL607 - ROBOTICS AND MACHINE VISION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Avoidance for a Differential Drive Robot Using Ultrasonic Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Drive Robot (DDR):** A non-holonomic mobile robot platform whose motion is achieved through the independent control of two coaxial drive wheels mounted on a common axle, with movement generated by the differential velocity (ωᵣ - ωₗ) between the left and right wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Obstacle Avoidance:** A reactive autonomous navigation paradigm in which a robot continuously perceives its environment using exteroceptive sensors (such as ultrasonic transducers) and modulates its trajectory in real time to maintain a collision-free path, without requiring a globally consistent map.

The **HC-SR04 ultrasonic ranging module** serves as the principal exteroceptive sensor in this experiment. It operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle, emitting a **40 kHz** acoustic burst and measuring the round-trip echo duration to infer target distance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Blindfolded Bat" Analogy**
> Imagine walking through a dark room with a walking stick. You tap the stick forward, listen for the echo, and if the echo returns quickly, you know a wall is close — so you turn. A differential drive robot equipped with an ultrasonic sensor behaves in the same way. The "stick" is the **40 kHz sound pulse**, the "echo" is the reflected wave, and the "turning decision" is the **reactive control law** executed by the microcontroller. Unlike your eyes, however, the robot is restricted to a single narrow sensing cone (~15°), so it must rotate its "head" (sensor) or its whole body to scan the surroundings.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** **c = 343 m/s** (or equivalently **0.0343 cm/μs**)
- **HC-SR04 operating frequency:** **40 kHz**
- **HC-SR04 effective range:** **2 cm to 400 cm** (with ±3 cm accuracy)
- **Standard trigger pulse duration:** **10 μs** (TTL level)
- **Threshold distance for avoidance (typical):** **d_th = 20 cm** (course-dependent)
- **Angular resolution of single HC-SR04:** **≈ 15°** conical beam
- **Differential drive wheel separation (typical lab chassis):** **L = 15–20 cm**
- **Wheel radius (typical):** **r = 3–4 cm**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PCRUL607 lab explicitly tests the integration of (a) sensor interfacing via GPIO/Arduino/STM32, (b) PWM-based DC motor actuation, and (c) a closed-loop reactive algorithm — all of which must be demonstrated live and recorded in the lab record.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Three Pillars

The experiment rests on three interlocking technical pillars:

**Pillar I — Time-of-Flight Distance Measurement**
1. The microcontroller issues a **10 μs TTL trigger pulse** to the HC-SR04 TRIG pin.
2. The sensor internally excites a piezo transducer, emitting **8 bursts of 40 kHz ultrasound**.
3. The transducer is then switched to receiver mode.
4. The microcontroller measures the width of the HIGH pulse on the ECHO pin (denoted **t_echo** in microseconds).
5. Distance is computed via the round-trip acoustic propagation equation.

**Pillar II — Differential Drive Kinematics**
1. The robot's pose in the world frame is denoted **(x, y, θ)**.
2. Two independent DC motors drive the left and right wheels at linear velocities **v_L** and **v_R**.
3. The forward kinematics maps wheel velocities to body twist **(v, ω)** using the wheel radius **r** and axle length **L**.
4. The **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)** lies on the line of the axle, perpendicular to it.

**Pillar III — Reactive Avoidance Control Law**
1. Sensor reading `d` is compared to threshold `d_th`.
2. If `d > d_th` → drive forward (straight line, v_L = v_R = v_nom).
3. If `d ≤ d_th` → execute avoidance maneuver (stop → reverse briefly → rotate).
4. The cycle repeats at a fixed control period (typically **50–100 ms**).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Constants | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Distance from echo time | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$ | c = 343 m/s, t in s, d in m | Round-trip factor of 2 |
| 2 | Distance (practical) | $d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{echo}}}{58}$ | t_echo in μs, d in cm | Equivalent to c/2 ≈ 0.0343/2 cm/μs × 1/0.0343 → 1/58.8 |
| 3 | Distance (alt.) | $d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{echo}}}{29 \cdot 2}$ | — | Use 29 for inch × 2 factor (sometimes 148 for inch) |
| 4 | Wheel linear velocity | $v = r \cdot \omega$ | r in m, ω in rad/s | v_L = r·ω_L, v_R = r·ω_R |
| 5 | Body linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | m/s | Average of wheel speeds |
| 6 | Body angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | rad/s | L = wheel separation |
| 7 | Radius of curvature | $R = \dfrac{L \cdot (v_R + v_L)}{2 \cdot (v_R - v_L)}$ | m | Diverges when v_R = v_L (straight line) |
| 8 | Speed of sound (temp-corrected) | $c = 331.3 \cdot \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273.15}}$ | T in °C, c in m/s | ~0.6 m/s per °C |
| 9 | PWM duty cycle (approx.) | $\text{duty} = \dfrac{v_{\text{desired}}}{v_{\text{max}}} \cdot 255$ | — | For 8-bit Arduino PWM |
| 10 | Beam spread (HC-SR04) | $\theta_{\text{beam}} \approx 15°$ | — | Conical, half-angle ≈ 7.5° |
| 11 | Minimum trigger period | $T_{\text{min}} \approx 60$ ms | — | Prevents echo collision from previous pulse |
| 12 | Control loop period | $T_{\text{ctrl}} = 50$–$100$ ms | — | Trade-off: latency vs. noise |

> [!WARNING]
> **Speed of Sound Trap:** Examiners frequently set problem variations where the room temperature differs from 20°C. Always re-evaluate **c** using the temperature-corrected formula before computing distance. A 10°C error in T produces approximately a **1.8% error in c** — significant when the threshold is tight.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Differential drive robots with ultrasonic arrays are foundational in **warehouse automation (AGVs)**, **floor-cleaning robots (iRobot Roomba)**, **hospital medication delivery bots**, and **agricultural field scouts**. The HC-SR04's low cost (~$1) makes it a prototyping favorite, though production systems migrate to **LiDAR** or **time-of-flight cameras** for full 360° coverage. Mastering this experiment builds the intuition for transitioning to those advanced systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Distance from Echo Time

We begin with the one-dimensional wave-propagation principle.

$$
\text{Step 1: Total round-trip distance covered by the sound wave} = c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: This is twice the one-way distance to the obstacle, so } 2d = c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Solving for } d \text{ yields the canonical ToF equation}
$$

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Substituting } c = 343 \text{ m/s} = 0.0343 \text{ cm/}\mu\text{s and } t_{\text{echo}} \text{ in } \mu\text{s}
$$

$$
d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{0.0343 \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2} = \frac{t_{\text{echo}}}{58.3}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: The integer-rounded, hardware-friendly form (Arduino ecosystem)} \Rightarrow d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{t_{\text{echo}}}{58}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Dividing the echo time (in μs) by 58 directly gives centimeters — a heuristic that absorbs the 2× round-trip factor and the unit conversion of the speed of sound simultaneously. This is the form most commonly embedded in `pulseIn()` based code.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Drive Forward Kinematics

Let the left and right wheels have angular velocities **ω_L** and **ω_R** (in rad/s), wheel radius **r**, and the axle (track) length be **L**.

$$
\text{Step 1: Linear velocity of each wheel}
$$

$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L \quad \text{and} \quad v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: The body linear velocity is the midpoint of the wheel velocities}
$$

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: The body angular velocity is the differential velocity divided by the axle length}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Deriving the radius of curvature from the ICC geometry}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R + \frac{L}{2} &= \frac{v_R}{\omega} \quad \text{(right wheel lies on the ICC circle)} \\
R - \frac{L}{2} &= \frac{v_L}{\omega} \quad \text{(left wheel lies on the ICC circle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: Adding the two equations}
$$

$$
2R = \frac{v_R + v_L}{\omega} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2\omega} = \frac{L \cdot (v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}
$$

> **Edge Case Analysis:** When **v_R = v_L**, the denominator vanishes and **R → ∞** — the robot moves in a perfect straight line (pure translation, no rotation). When **v_L = -v_R**, the robot rotates about its own ICC, which coincides with the midpoint of the axle (zero-radius turn).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed of Sound at a Given Temperature

The speed of sound in dry air depends on the absolute temperature **T** (in Kelvin) per the Laplace formula.

$$
c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T}{M}}
$$

$$
\text{Step 1: For diatomic air, } \gamma = 1.4, \ R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}, \ M = 0.02897 \text{ kg/mol}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: This reduces to the well-known engineering approximation}
$$

$$
c \approx 331.3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{273.15}} \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Substituting } T = 293.15 \text{ K (i.e., 20°C)}
$$

$$
c = 331.3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{293.15}{273.15}} = 331.3 \cdot 1.0730 \approx 343.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: A useful rule of thumb}
$$

$$
c(T) \approx 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T_{^\circ\text{C}} \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

> **Example numeric check:** At **30°C**, c ≈ 331.3 + 0.606(30) = **349.5 m/s**, a +1.9% shift relative to 20°C. Lab examinations often test this.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behavior of the DDR under Various Wheel Command Pairs

| Command (ω_L, ω_R) | Linear velocity v | Angular velocity ω | Geometric interpretation |
|--------------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| (ω, ω) | r·ω | 0 | Pure forward translation; ICC at infinity |
| (0, 0) | 0 | 0 | Robot stationary |
| (ω, -ω) | 0 | 2r·ω / L | Pure in-place rotation (zero-radius turn) |
| (ω, 0) | r·ω / 2 | r·ω / L | Right wheel pinned → robot arcs left |
| (0, ω) | r·ω / 2 | -r·ω / L | Left wheel pinned → robot arcs right |
| (ω, 2ω) | 1.5r·ω | r·ω / L | Gentle right arc, R = 1.5L |
| (-ω, ω) | 0 | -2r·ω / L | In-place clockwise rotation |

> [!TIP]
> **Algorithm Insight:** The obstacle-avoidance maneuver in the lab typically uses a three-stage reactive command: **(a) reverse both wheels** (v_L = v_R = -v_rev), **(b) stop**, and **(c) rotate one wheel** (e.g., ω_L > 0, ω_R = 0) to pivot the robot away from the obstacle. Choosing the **direction of rotation** based on which side has more clearance is a common Part B question variation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Hardware Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of the experimental setup — sensor, controller, actuator, and the feedback loop that constitutes the reactive control cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DC Power Supply 7.4V Li-ion] --> A2[Motor Driver L298N]
    A2 --> A3[Left DC Motor]
    A2 --> A4[Right DC Motor]
    A3 --> A5[Differential Drive Chassis]
    A4 --> A5
    B1[Arduino UNO / STM32] -->|PWM ENA, ENB| A2
    B1 -->|IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4| A2
    C1[HC-SR04 VCC 5V] --> B1
    C2[HC-SR04 GND] --> B1
    C3[HC-SR04 TRIG Pin 9] --> B1
    C4[HC-SR04 ECHO Pin 10] --> B1
    D1[Ultrasonic Burst 40 kHz] --> C1
    C1 --> D2[Reflected Echo from Obstacle]
    D2 --> C4
    B1 --> E1[Serial Monitor - Distance Log]
    B1 --> E2[Decision Logic - Threshold Check]
    E2 --> A2
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — Reactive Obstacle Avoidance

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart captures the exact **Sense → Decide → Act** cycle executed in the firmware. It is the centerpiece of any viva or record-book explanation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1([Power ON / Reset]) --> P2[Initialize GPIO and PWM]
    P2 --> P3[Set motors to STOP]
    P3 --> P4{Send 10 us TRIG pulse to HC-SR04}
    P4 --> P5[Measure ECHO HIGH width via pulseIn]
    P5 --> P6[Compute distance d = t_echo / 58 cm]
    P6 --> P7{d greater than d_threshold}
    P7 -->|YES| P8[Drive FORWARD - both wheels forward PWM]
    P8 --> P9[Delay 100 ms]
    P9 --> P4
    P7 -->|NO| P10[STOP both motors - brake]
    P10 --> P11[REVERSE for 300 ms]
    P11 --> P12[STOP - brief pause 100 ms]
    P12 --> P13[ROTATE right - left wheel forward, right wheel stop]
    P13 --> P14[Delay proportional to obstacle size]
    P14 --> P4
```

### 4.3 Differential Drive Kinematic Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic below illustrates the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)** for a generic differential drive, showing the relationship between wheel velocities and the resulting circular arc trajectory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Global Frame XY] --> G2[Robot Pose x, y, theta]
    G2 --> G3[Right Wheel Velocity v_R]
    G2 --> G4[Left Wheel Velocity v_L]
    G3 --> G5[ICC Geometry]
    G4 --> G5
    G5 --> G6[Radius of Curvature R]
    G5 --> G7[Body Twist v and omega]
    G6 --> G8[Arc Trajectory in World Frame]
    G7 --> G8
```

### 4.4 HC-SR04 Pinout and Wiring Reference Table

| HC-SR04 Pin | Function | Connected To (Arduino UNO) | Notes |
|-------------|----------|----------------------------|-------|
| VCC | +5 V supply | 5 V rail | Do not power from 3.3 V logic MCU without level shift |
| GND | Ground | GND | Common ground with motors |
| TRIG | Trigger input (10 μs TTL pulse) | Digital Pin 9 (output) | Initialize as OUTPUT |
| ECHO | Echo output (TTL HIGH pulse) | Digital Pin 10 (input) | Use voltage divider (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) for 3.3 V MCUs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Level-Shifting Note:** The HC-SR04 echoes a **5 V** signal, which exceeds the **3.3 V** tolerance of ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico, and some STM32 pins. A simple resistive divider (R1 = 1 kΩ, R2 = 2 kΩ) scales the 5 V echo to ~3.3 V, preserving the high-level logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**State the principle of operation of the HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor. Mention any two of its electrical specifications.**

**Model Answer:**
The HC-SR04 operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle. A **10 μs TTL trigger pulse** applied to its TRIG pin causes the internal transducer to emit **eight cycles of 40 kHz ultrasound**. The transducer switches to receiver mode and raises the ECHO pin HIGH for a duration **t_echo** proportional to the round-trip travel time of the acoustic burst. The microcontroller computes distance as **d = (c · t_echo) / 2**, where **c ≈ 343 m/s**.

Two electrical specifications:
1. Operating voltage: **+5 V DC**, with operating current of **15 mA**.
2. Effective measurement range: **2 cm to 400 cm** with **±3 cm** accuracy; beam angle ≈ **15°**.

> **Marking pattern:** Principle — 1 mark, equation — 1 mark, two specs — 1 mark (0.5 each).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
**What is a differential drive robot? Why is it classified as a non-holonomic system?**

**Model Answer:**
A differential drive robot is a wheeled mobile platform that uses **two independently controlled coaxial drive wheels** to achieve planar motion. By varying the velocity difference (ω_R − ω_L), the robot can translate, rotate, or follow an arc.

It is **non-holonomic** because the **controllable degrees of freedom (2: v, ω) are fewer than the total configuration-space degrees of freedom (3: x, y, θ)**. Equivalently, the system has a non-integrable velocity constraint — the robot cannot move sideways (pure lateral translation is impossible), even though it has 3 generalized coordinates. Mathematically, the sideways velocity $\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0$.

> **Marking pattern:** Definition — 1 mark, kinematic equation or constraint — 1 mark, intuitive explanation — 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing v and ω derivation:** Many students invert the forward and inverse kinematic relations. Memorize: **forward** = wheel speeds → body twist; **inverse** = body twist → wheel speeds.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF:** Omitting the round-trip factor gives **double the true distance**. Examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring beam geometry:** Stating a distance reading for an obstacle **outside the 15° cone** is meaningless. Mention this when justifying sensor placement.
> 4. **No diagram in the kinematic answer:** A 14-marker almost always requires a labeled diagram of the DDR geometry. Missing it costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 5. **Mixing up clockwise/counter-clockwise conventions:** Be consistent with the right-hand rule or explicitly state your sign convention.

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Derive the forward kinematics of a differential drive robot. Express the body twist (v, ω) in terms of wheel angular velocities (ω_L, ω_R), wheel radius r, and axle length L. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius r = 4 cm and wheel separation L = 18 cm. The left wheel rotates at ω_L = 5 rad/s and the right wheel at ω_R = 10 rad/s. Compute the linear velocity v, angular velocity ω, and the radius of curvature R of the path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [Geometric derivation as in Section 3.2]
Let the wheels have angular velocities ω_L and ω_R. The linear velocities at the contact points are v_L = r·ω_L and v_R = r·ω_R. The body linear velocity v is the average:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

The body angular velocity is the differential velocity divided by axle length:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

[Include a labeled figure showing the ICC perpendicular to the axle.]

**(b)** Given: r = 0.04 m, L = 0.18 m, ω_L = 5 rad/s, ω_R = 10 rad/s.

Step 1: Compute wheel linear velocities.
v_L = 0.04 × 5 = **0.20 m/s**
v_R = 0.04 × 10 = **0.40 m/s**

Step 2: Body linear velocity.
v = (0.40 + 0.20) / 2 = **0.30 m/s** (forward)

Step 3: Body angular velocity.
ω = (0.40 − 0.20) / 0.18 = **1.111 rad/s** (counter-clockwise)

Step 4: Radius of curvature.
R = (v_R + v_L) / (2ω) = (0.40 + 0.20) / (2 × 1.111) = 0.60 / 2.222 = **0.27 m** (≈ 27 cm)

> **Verification:** A right wheel faster than the left ⇒ robot curves **left** (counter-clockwise). The ICC lies **0.27 m to the left** of the robot's center, consistent with our positive ω.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** Explain the Time-of-Flight principle used by the HC-SR04. Derive the distance equation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The echo pin of an HC-SR04 reads a HIGH pulse of width **1170 μs** at a room temperature of **30°C**. Compute the distance to the obstacle in centimeters. Justify any temperature correction you apply. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [Derivation as in Section 3.1, with c = 343 m/s and the factor of 2 explained.]

**(b)** Step 1: Correct speed of sound for 30°C.
c(30°C) = 331.3 + 0.606 × 30 = 331.3 + 18.18 = **349.48 m/s**
or equivalently, c = 349.48 × 100 / 1,000,000 cm/μs = **3.4948 × 10⁻² cm/μs**

Step 2: Apply ToF equation.
d = (c · t_echo) / 2 = (3.4948 × 10⁻² × 1170) / 2

d = (40.889) / 2 = **20.44 cm**

Step 3: Compare with the naïve 20°C value.
Using c = 0.0343 cm/μs, d = (0.0343 × 1170) / 2 = 40.131 / 2 = **20.07 cm**

Step 4: Quantify the error.
Δd = 20.44 − 20.07 = 0.37 cm, which is **~1.85%** of the true distance — well above the sensor's ±3 cm accuracy floor, so the correction is meaningful in precision-critical applications.

> **Justification:** The 1.85% shift, while small, accumulates in navigation stacks where dead-reckoning is used. A robot traveling 10 m would accumulate ~18.5 cm of range error purely from this temperature drift.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Save This for the Night Before the Lab Exam**

- [x] **HC-SR04 trigger pulse width:** Exactly **10 μs** (use `digitalWrite(trig, HIGH); delayMicroseconds(10);`).
- [x] **Distance formula:** **d = t_echo / 58** (cm, μs) — the factor of 58 hides both the round-trip and the speed of sound.
- [x] **Minimum cycle time:** Wait at least **60 ms** between consecutive trigger pulses to prevent echo collisions.
- [x] **Speed of sound correction:** **c = 331.3 + 0.606·T(°C)** m/s — always recompute for non-standard lab temperatures.
- [x] **Beam cone:** ~**15°** conical; obstacles outside this cone are invisible to a single static sensor.
- [x] **Forward kinematics:**
    - v = (v_R + v_L) / 2
    - ω = (v_R − v_L) / L
    - R = L·(v_R + v_L) / [2·(v_R − v_L)]
- [x] **Special cases:** (ω_L = ω_R) ⇒ straight line; (ω_L = −ω_R) ⇒ zero-radius in-place turn; (ω_L = 0 or ω_R = 0) ⇒ arc pivoting about the stationary wheel.
- [x] **Non-holonomic constraint:** A differential drive **cannot** move sideways — this is the geometric reason for the constraint $\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0$.
- [x] **Reactive algorithm skeleton:** `read_distance()` → `compare_to_threshold()` → `actuate_motors(PWM, direction)`. This is the *Sense → Decide → Act* loop.
- [x] **Motor driver wiring:** L298N (or L293D) needs **ENA/ENB PWM pins** for speed and **IN1–IN4** for direction; never connect motor terminals directly to MCU GPIO.
- [x] **Level shifting:** Use a **1 kΩ + 2 kΩ** resistor divider on the ECHO pin if interfacing to a **3.3 V** MCU.
- [x] **Viva favorite:** "What is the **maximum distance** the HC-SR04 can measure?" — answer: **400 cm** (with the practical caveat that this degrades for soft/absorbent surfaces like cloth).
- [x] **Viva favorite:** "What happens if **v_R = v_L ≠ 0**?" — answer: the robot translates in a **straight line**; ICC lies at infinity; ω = 0.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization of a Mobile Robot Using LIDAR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL607 - ROBOTICS AND MACHINE VISION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Localization of a Mobile Robot Using LIDAR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mobile Robot Localization** is the process of estimating the **pose** (position $x, y$ and orientation $\theta$) of a robot within a globally referenced coordinate frame, using **LIDAR** (Light Detection and Ranging) sensor measurements and a prior known map of the environment.

In the context of KTU's PCRUL607 lab framework, **LIDAR-based localization** is formally defined as a recursive state-estimation problem where the robot's belief about its pose is updated by fusing:

1. **Control inputs** $u_t$ (odometry / wheel encoder data) — the *motion model*.
2. **Observations** $z_t$ (LIDAR range scans) — the *measurement / sensor model*.
3. **A prior map** $m$ of the operating environment.

The mathematical goal is to compute the posterior belief:

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)
$$

where $x_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^T$ is the robot pose at time $t$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Blind Person in a Familiar Room":**
> Imagine a person blindfolded inside their own bedroom (a map they know by heart). They walk a few steps (using their inner ear + muscle sense → odometry). Then they snap their fingers and listen for echoes (LIDAR scan). By matching the echo pattern to what they *know* the room looks like ("the wall is 1.5 m to my left"), they localize themselves.
> - **Finger snaps** = LIDAR laser pulses.
> - **Echo pattern** = Range scan $(r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n)$.
> - **Mental map of the room** = Prior occupancy grid map.
> - **Steps taken** = Odometry input $u_t$.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum/air:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ — the fundamental constant underlying all LIDAR range measurement.
- **Typical LIDAR angular resolution:** $\Delta\theta = 0.25^\circ$ to $1.0^\circ$.
- **Range scan rate:** $f_s = 10$ to $40$ Hz.
- **Maximum effective range:** $r_{\max} = 25$ to $100$ m for outdoor units.
- **Localization accuracy benchmark (KTU lab):** position error $\epsilon_p < 0.05$ m; orientation error $\epsilon_\theta < 2^\circ$.

### 1.4 Why Localization Matters in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> Without accurate localization, a robot cannot perform **path planning**, **obstacle avoidance**, or **task execution**. Localization is the *first* downstream consumer of perception data and the *last* upstream provider of state to the controller. This is why KTU devotes a dedicated experiment to it in Module 2 of the Robotics and Machine Vision Lab.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LIDAR Operating Principle — Time of Flight (ToF)

A LIDAR sensor emits a laser pulse at time $t_e$ in a known direction $\phi$. The pulse strikes an obstacle and a portion of the energy returns to the receiver at time $t_r$. The round-trip distance is:

- **Step 1 — Emitted pulse** travels at speed of light $c$.
- **Step 2 — Distance calculation:** the pulse covers the round-trip distance $2r$ in time $\Delta t = t_r - t_e$.
- **Step 3 — Solve for range:** $r = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$.

The sensor sweeps the laser across an angular range (typically $180^\circ$ or $360^\circ$), producing a **scan** $z_t = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_N\}$ where $N$ is the number of beams.

### 2.2 The Localization Pipeline (Why and How)

The localization algorithm fuses two probabilistic models:

**A. Motion Model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ — How the robot moves.**
Given an odometry input $u_t = (\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)^T$ from wheel encoders, the predicted pose is:

$$
x_t = \begin{pmatrix} x_{t-1} \\ y_{t-1} \\ \theta_{t-1} \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} \Delta x \cos\theta_{t-1} - \Delta y \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ \Delta x \sin\theta_{t-1} + \Delta y \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ \Delta \theta \end{pmatrix} + \varepsilon_t
$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ is zero-mean Gaussian process noise with covariance $R_t$.

**B. Measurement Model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ — How the robot senses.**
Given a hypothesized pose $x_t$, the algorithm ray-casts from the LIDAR origin through every beam direction $\phi_i$ into the map $m$, and predicts the expected range $\hat{r}_i$. The actual measurement $r_i$ is compared to $\hat{r}_i$ via a Gaussian likelihood:

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{N}\!\left(r_i;\ \hat{r}_i(x_t, m),\ \sigma_r^2\right)
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU ESE answers on this topic. Memorize the units and conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | LIDAR Range (ToF) | $r = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time | m, s |
| 2 | Pose Vector | $x_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^T$ | 2D position + heading | m, m, rad |
| 3 | Belief (Bayes Filter) | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Posterior estimate over pose | probability density |
| 4 | Motion Update | $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t$ | $f$ = nonlinear kinematic function | m, rad |
| 5 | Measurement Likelihood | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_i \mathcal{N}(r_i;\ \hat{r}_i, \sigma_r^2)$ | Product of independent Gaussian beams | scalar |
| 6 | EKF Prediction Covariance | $\bar{P}_t = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | Uncertainty propagation | $\text{m}^2, \text{rad}^2$ |
| 7 | EKF Update Gain | $K_t = \bar{P}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{P}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain matrix | dimensionless |
| 8 | ICP Rotation Matrix | $R = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ | 2D planar rotation | dimensionless |
| 9 | Particle Weight | $w_t^{[k]} = w_{t-1}^{[k]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[k]}, m)$ | Importance weight for particle $k$ | scalar |
| 10 | RMSE Error | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_{true,k} - x_{est,k})^2}$ | Localization accuracy metric | m or rad |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

LIDAR-based localization is the backbone of:
- **Autonomous vehicles** (Google/Waymo, Tesla FSD, Indian defence AGVs).
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva robots).
- **Service robots** (hospital delivery, restaurant servers).
- **Disaster-response robots** (DRDO Daksh, Boston Dynamics Spot).
- **Agricultural robots** (precision spraying, mapping).

The algorithm chosen (EKF vs. Particle Filter vs. Scan Matching) depends on environment structure and computational budget.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — LIDAR Range from Time of Flight

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **fundamental derivation** that KTU examiners love to set as a 3-mark sub-part. Memorize every line.

**Given:** A laser pulse is emitted at time $t_e$, reflected by a wall, and received at time $t_r$. The pulse travels at speed $c$ in air.

**Step 1 — Express the round-trip distance traveled by light.**
The light travels from emitter to wall and back, covering a total distance $d_{round}$:

$$
d_{round} = c \cdot (t_r - t_e) = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 2 — Relate $d_{round}$ to the one-way range $r$.**
Since the light must travel *to* the wall (distance $r$) and *back* (distance $r$), the round-trip distance is twice the one-way range:

$$
d_{round} = 2r
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $d_{round}$.**

$$
2r = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $r$.**

$$
r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check with units.**

$$
[r] = \frac{[\text{m/s}] \cdot [\text{s}]}{1} = \text{m}
$$

Units check out. For a typical $\Delta t = 6.67$ ns (return from an object 1 m away), we verify:

$$
r = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (6.67 \times 10^{-9})}{2} = \frac{2.001}{2} = 1.0005 \text{ m}
$$

This is the **exact** result — confirming the derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Bayes Filter Recursion for Localization

The probability that the robot is at pose $x_t$ given all measurements and controls up to time $t$ is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Start with Bayes' rule applied to the posterior.**

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $\eta$ is the normalization constant.

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption (current measurement depends only on current state).**

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the prior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ via the Chapman–Kolmogorov equation by marginalizing over $x_{t-1}$.**

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two steps and recognize the recursion.**

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This is the **Bayes Filter Localization Equation** — the unifying equation from which the Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, and EKF are derived.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — EKF Linearization for LIDAR Localization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The EKF linearizes the nonlinear motion and measurement models using **first-order Taylor expansion** (Jacobian).

**Step 1 — Motion model (nonlinear).**

$$
x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Linearize $g$ around the current mean $\mu_{t-1}$.**

$$
g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})
$$

where $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x_{t-1}}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$ is the Jacobian.

**Step 3 — Predict the new mean (deterministic part).**

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Predict the new covariance.**

$$
\bar{P}_t = G_t P_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

**Step 5 — Linearize the measurement model $h(x_t) = \hat{r}_i(x_t)$.**

$$
h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

where $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x_t}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ is the measurement Jacobian.

**Step 6 — Compute Kalman Gain.**

$$
K_t = \bar{P}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{P}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Update the mean with the innovation $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$.**

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t
$$

**Step 8 — Update the covariance.**

$$
P_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{P}_t
$$

This is the **complete EKF localization update** for one LIDAR scan.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter) Update

**Step 1 — Initialize a set of $M$ particles $S_t = \{x_t^{[1]}, x_t^{[2]}, \dots, x_t^{[M]}\}$ drawn from the prior belief.**

$$
x_0^{[k]} \sim p(x_0), \quad w_0^{[k]} = \frac{1}{M}
$$

**Step 2 — For each particle, sample a new pose using the motion model.**

$$
x_t^{[k]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[k]}, u_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the importance weight using the measurement likelihood.**

$$
w_t^{[k]} = w_{t-1}^{[k]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[k]}, m)
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize the weights so they sum to one.**

$$
\tilde{w}_t^{[k]} = \frac{w_t^{[k]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}
$$

**Step 5 — Resample: replace weak particles with copies of strong particles (with replacement).**

$$
S_t = \text{Resample}(S_{t-1}, \tilde{w}_t)
$$

**Step 6 — The pose estimate is the weighted mean of the particles.**

$$
\hat{x}_t = \sum_{k=1}^{M} \tilde{w}_t^{[k]} \cdot x_t^{[k]}
$$

This is **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)**, the most popular algorithm in KTU-style lab implementations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level LIDAR Localization Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Robot Start Pose x0"] --> A1["Receive LIDAR Scan zt"]
    A1 --> A2["Receive Odometry ut"]
    A2 --> B1["Motion Model: Predict Pose"]
    B1 --> B2["Sample Particles / Propagate EKF"]
    B2 --> C1["Ray Casting into Map m"]
    C1 --> C2["Compute Predicted Ranges"]
    C2 --> D1["Compare Predicted vs Measured"]
    D1 --> D2["Compute Likelihood p(zt vert xt, m)"]
    D2 --> E1["Weight Particles / Kalman Update"]
    E1 --> E2{"Resample or Convergence?"}
    E2 -->|"No"| B1
    E2 -->|"Yes"| F1["Output Estimated Pose xt_hat"]
    F1 --> A1
```

### 4.2 Sensor and Coordinate Frame Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR["LIDAR Sensor Unit"]
        L1["Laser Emitter"]
        L2["Rotating Mirror / MEMS"]
        L3["Photodetector Receiver"]
        L4["Time to Digital Converter"]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> M1["Range Vector zt"]
    M1 --> ALGO["Localization Algorithm"]
    subgraph ALGO["Bayes Filter Module"]
        M2["Motion Model"]
        M3["Measurement Model"]
        M4["Belief Update"]
    end
    ODO["Wheel Encoders / IMU"] --> M2
    MAP["Prior Occupancy Grid Map m"] --> M3
    M2 --> M4
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> OUT["Estimated Pose xt_hat"]
    OUT --> PLAN["Path Planner / Controller"]
```

### 4.3 Particle Filter Resampling Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Previous Particles St-1 with weights w_t-1"] --> P1["Predict: xt_k ~ p(xt vert xt-1_k, ut)"]
    P1 --> P2["Measure: receive LIDAR scan zt"]
    P2 --> P3["Score: wk = p(zt vert xt_k, m)"]
    P3 --> P4["Normalize weights w_k_tilde"]
    P4 --> P5["Resample: draw M particles proportional to weights"]
    P5 --> P6["New Particle Set St"]
    P6 --> P7["Pose Estimate: weighted mean"]
```

### 4.4 ICP Scan Matching Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ICP0["Reference Scan Sref from Map"] --> ICP1["Transform Source Scan by T"]
    ICP1 --> ICP2["Find Correspondences: nearest neighbors"]
    ICP2 --> ICP3["Compute Optimal Rotation R and Translation t"]
    ICP3 --> ICP4{"Error below threshold?"}
    ICP4 -->|"No"| ICP5["Update T = T plus delta_T"]
    ICP5 --> ICP1
    ICP4 -->|"Yes"| ICP6["Output Aligned Transform T_star"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define LIDAR-based localization. State any two advantages of using LIDAR over vision-based localization.*
